name: shandy-sqlfmt

metadata.name: shandy-sqlfmt
classifiers: License :: OSI Approved :: Apache Software License
metadata.summary: sqlfmt formats your dbt SQL files so you don't have to.

documentation:
# sqlfmt

[![PyPI](https://img.shields.io/pypi/v/shandy-sqlfmt)](https://pypi.org/project/shandy-sqlfmt/)
[![Downloads](https://static.pepy.tech/personalized-badge/shandy-sqlfmt?period=month&units=international_system&left_color=grey&right_color=orange&left_text=downloads/mo)](https://pepy.tech/project/shandy-sqlfmt)
[![Test](https://github.com/tconbeer/sqlfmt/actions/workflows/test.yml/badge.svg?branch=main&event=push)](https://github.com/tconbeer/sqlfmt/actions/workflows/test.yml)

![PyPI - Python Version](https://img.shields.io/pypi/pyversions/shandy-sqlfmt)
![Runs on Linux | MacOS | Windows](https://img.shields.io/badge/runs%20on-Linux%20%7C%20MacOS%20%7C%20Windows-blue)


sqlfmt formats your dbt SQL files so you don't have to. It is similar in nature to black, gofmt, 
and rustfmt (but for SQL). 

1. **sqlfmt promotes collaboration.** An auto-formatter makes it easier to collaborate with your team and solicit contributions from new people. You will never have to mention (or argue about) code style in code reviews again.
2. **sqlfmt is fast.** Forget about formatting your code, and spend your time on business logic instead. sqlfmt processes hundreds of files per second and only operates on files that have changed since the last run.
3. **sqlfmt works with Jinja.** It formats the code that users look at, and therefore doesn't need to know anything about what happens after the templates are rendered.
3. **sqlfmt integrates with your workflow.** As a CLI written in Python, it's easy to install locally on any OS and run in CI. Plays well with dbt, pre-commit, SQLFluff, VSCode, and GitHub Actions. sqlfmt powers the dbt Cloud IDE's Format button.

sqlfmt is not configurable, except for line length. It enforces a single style. sqlfmt maintains comments and some extra newlines, but largely ignores all indentation and line breaks in the input file.

sqlfmt is not a linter. It does not parse your code into an AST; it just lexes it and tracks a small subset of tokens that impact formatting. This lets us "do one thing and do it well:" sqlfmt is very fast, and easier to maintain and extend than linters that need a full SQL grammar.

For now, sqlfmt only works on `select`, `delete`, `grant`, `revoke`, and `create function` statements (which is all you need if you use sqlfmt with a dbt project). It is being extended to additional DDL and DML. Visit [this tracking issue](https://github.com/tconbeer/sqlfmt/issues/262) for more information.

## Documentation

Please visit [docs.sqlfmt.com](https://docs.sqlfmt.com) for more information on Getting Started, Integrations, the sqlfmt Style, and an API Reference. Or keep reading for an excerpt from the full docs.

### Installation

#### Try it first
Want to test out sqlfmt on a query before you install it? Go to [sqlfmt.com](https://sqlfmt.com) to use the interactive, web-based version.

#### Install Using pipx (recommended)
sqlfmt is a pip-installable Python package listed on PyPI under the name `shandy-sqlfmt`. You should install it into a virtual environment, which `pipx` does automatically:

```
pipx install shandy-sqlfmt
```

To install with the jinjafmt extra (which will also install the Python code formatter, *black*):

```
pipx install shandy-sqlfmt[jinjafmt]
```

For more installation options, [read the docs](https://docs.sqlfmt.com/getting-started/installation).

### Getting Started

#### Other prerequisites
**sqlfmt will not always produce the formatted output you might want.** It might even break your SQL syntax. It is **highly recommended** to only run sqlfmt on files in a version control system (like git), so that it is easy for you to revert any changes made by sqlfmt. On your first run, be sure to make a commit before running sqlfmt.

There are certain situations where sqlfmt can be considered to be in Beta, or even more mature than that. Those are:

1. Using sqlfmt to format select statements for one of the major dialects (PostgresSQL, MySQL, Snowflake, BQ, Redshift).

1. Using sqlfmt to format a dbt project (which may also include jinja and some minimal DDL/DML, like grants, create function, etc.) for one of the major dialects.

However, there are other use cases where sqlfmt is very much alpha:

1. Formatting some dialects that deviate from ANSI or Postgres, like T-SQL (SQLServer).

1. Formatting other DDL (create table, insert, etc.) (sqlfmt attempts to be no-op on these statements as much as possible).

In these domains sqlfmt is nowhere near "feature complete" and caution is highly advised.

#### Using sqlfmt
To list commands and options:

```bash
sqlfmt --help
```

If you want to format all `.sql` and `.sql.jinja` files in your current working directory (and all nested directories), simply type:
```bash
$ sqlfmt .
```

If you don't want to format the files you have on disk, you can run sqlfmt with the `--check` option. sqlfmt will exit with code 1 if the files on disk are not properly formatted:
```bash
$ sqlfmt --check .
```
If you want to print a diff of changes that sqlfmt would make to format a file (but not update the file on disk), you can use the `--diff` option. `--diff` also exits with 1 on changes:
```bash
$ sqlfmt --diff .
```

For more commands, see [the docs](https://docs.sqlfmt.com/getting-started/using-sqlfmt).

#### Configuring sqlfmt using pyproject.toml

Any command-line option for sqlfmt can also be set in a `pyproject.toml` file, under a `[tool.sqlfmt]` section header. Options passed at the command line will override the settings in the config file. [See the docs](https://docs.sqlfmt.com/getting-started/configuring-sqlfmt) for more information.

#### The jinjafmt extra

sqlfmt loves properly-formatted jinja, too.

[See the docs](https://docs.sqlfmt.com/getting-started/formatting-jinja) for more information about using the `jinjafmt` extra or disabling jinja formatting.

### Using sqlfmt with different SQL dialects

sqlfmt's rules are simple, which means it does not have to parse every single token in your query. This allows nearly all SQL dialects to be formatted using sqlfmt's default "polyglot" dialect, which requires no configuration.

The exception to this is [ClickHouse](https://docs.sqlfmt.com/dialects/#clickhouse), which is case-sensitive where other dialects are not. To prevent the lowercasing of function names, database identifiers, and aliases, use the `--dialect clickhouse` option when running sqlfmt. For example,

```bash
$ sqlfmt . --dialect clickhouse
```

This can also be configured using the `pyproject.toml` file:

```toml
[tool.sqlfmt]
dialect = "clickhouse"
```

Note that with this option, sqlfmt will not lowercase **most** non-reserved keywords, even common ones like `sum` or `count`. See (and please join) [this discussion](https://github.com/tconbeer/sqlfmt/discussions/229) for more on this topic.

### Integrations

sqlfmt plays nicely with other analytics engineering tools. For more information, [see the docs](https://docs.sqlfmt.com/category/integrations).

#### dbt

sqlfmt was built for dbt, so only [minimal configuration](https://docs.sqlfmt.com/integrations/dbt) is required. We recommend excluding your `target` and `dbt_packages` directories from formatting. You can do this with the command-line `--exclude` option, or by setting `exclude` in your `pyproject.toml` file:

```toml
[tool.sqlfmt]
exclude=["target/**/*", "dbt_packages/**/*"]
```

#### Other Integrations

Config for other integrations is detailed in the docs linked below:

- [pre-commit](https://docs.sqlfmt.com/integrations/pre-commit)
- [SQLFluff](https://docs.sqlfmt.com/integrations/sqlfluff)
- [VSCode](https://docs.sqlfmt.com/integrations/vs-code)


## The sqlfmt style
The only thing you can configure with sqlfmt is the desired line length of the formatted file. You can do this with the `--line-length` or `-l` options. The default is 88.

sqlfmt borrows elements from well-accepted styles from other programming languages. It places opening brackets on the same line as preceding function names (like *black* for python and *1TBS* for C). It indents closing brackets to the same depth as the opening bracket (this is extended to statements that must be closed, like `case` and `end`).

The sqlfmt style is as simple as possible, with little-to-no special-casing of formatting concerns. While at first blush, this may not create a format that is as "nice" or "expressive" as hand-crafted indentation, over time, as you grow accustomed to the style, formatting becomes transparent and the consistency will allow you to jump between files, projects, and even companies much faster.

[Read More](https://docs.sqlfmt.com/style/)

### Why lowercase?
Because SQL is code! But there are [other good reasons too](https://docs.sqlfmt.com/style/#why-lowercase).

### Why trailing commas?
Using trailing commas follows the convention of every other written language and programming language. [But wait, there's more.](https://docs.sqlfmt.com/style/#why-trailing-commas)

## Contributing

[![Code style: black](https://img.shields.io/badge/code%20style-black-000000.svg)](https://github.com/psf/black)
[![Checked with mypy](http://www.mypy-lang.org/static/mypy_badge.svg)](http://mypy-lang.org/)
[![Maintainability](https://api.codeclimate.com/v1/badges/8928f6662a67b8eaf092/maintainability)](https://codeclimate.com/github/tconbeer/sqlfmt/maintainability)
[![Test Coverage](https://api.codeclimate.com/v1/badges/8928f6662a67b8eaf092/test_coverage)](https://codeclimate.com/github/tconbeer/sqlfmt/test_coverage)

### Providing Feedback

We'd love to hear from you! [Open an Issue](https://github.com/tconbeer/sqlfmt/issues/new/choose) to request new features, report bad formatting, or say hello.

### Setting up Your Dev Environment and Running Tests

1. Install [Poetry](https://python-poetry.org/docs/#installation) v1.2 or higher if you don't have it already. You may also need or want pyenv, make, and gcc. A complete setup from a fresh install of Ubuntu can be found [here](https://github.com/tconbeer/linux_setup).
1. Clone this repo into a directory (let's call it `sqlfmt`), then `cd sqlfmt`.
1. Use `poetry install --all-extras --sync` to install the project (editable) and its dependencies (including the `jinjafmt` and `sqlfmt_primer` extras) into a new virtual env.
1. Use `poetry shell` to spawn a subshell.
1. Type `make` to run all tests and linters, or run `pytest`, `black`, `flake8`, `isort`, and `mypy` individually.

### Updating primer repos to reflect formatting changes

1. Make sure all changes are committed to sqlfmt.
1. Check out `main` in the repo and make sure you `pull` changes locally.
1. Check out the `unformatted` tag in the repo with `git checkout -b chore/apply-abc123 unformatted` where `abc123` is the hash of the most recent sqlfmt commit (from 1).
1. Run sqlfmt against the working tree, then `git add .` and `git commit -m "chore: apply sqlfmt abc123"`.
1. We will have conflicts with main that we want to ignore, so merge main into this branch, ignoring anything on main: `git merge -s ours main`.
1. Push and open a PR; squash and merge. Grab the commit SHA.
1. Paste the commit SHA as a ref into `primer.py`.
1. Run `sqlfmt_primer -k` to clear the cache, then update the stats in `primer.py` to match the results.

functions.name: sqlfmt.report.style_output
documentation:
A thin wrapper around click.style.

See https://click.palletsprojects.com/en/8.0.x/api/?highlight=style#click.style

functions.name: sqlfmt.report.unstyle_output
documentation: A thin wrapper around click.unstyle.

functions.name: sqlfmt.report.display_output
documentation: A thin wrapper around click.echo; defaults to printing to stderr.

classes.name: sqlfmt.report.SqlFormatResult

methods.name: sqlfmt.report.SqlFormatResult.maybe_print_to_stdout
documentation: If sqlfmt received a query via stdin, print the formatted string to stdout
A SqlfmtResult is a summary of the changes made by sqlfmt to a single file.

classes.name: sqlfmt.report.Report

methods.name: sqlfmt.report.Report.__str__
documentation: Returns the full contents of the Report

methods.name: sqlfmt.report.Report._pluralize_file
documentation: Returns either "1 file" or "n files", depending on n

methods.name: sqlfmt.report.Report._generate_diff
documentation:
Returns a non-colorized diff of the source and formatted
strings in the SqlfmtResult

methods.name: sqlfmt.report.Report._style_diff_line
documentation: Colorizes the diff created by _generate_diff

methods.name: sqlfmt.report.Report.display_report
documentation:
If sqlfmt received a query via stdin, print the formatted string to stdout.

Then print the report that summarizes all results

An abstraction for a summary of results generated by a sqlfmt run.
Can be printed to stderr using display_report()

functions.name: sqlfmt.rules.common.group
documentation: Convenience function for creating grouped alternatives in regex

functions.name: sqlfmt.actions.group
documentation: Convenience function for creating grouped alternatives in regex

functions.name: sqlfmt.actions.add_node_to_buffer
documentation:
Create a token of token_type from the match, then create a Node
from that token and append it to the Analyzer's buffer

functions.name: sqlfmt.actions.safe_add_node_to_buffer
documentation:
Try to create a token of token_type from the match; if that fails
with a SqlfmtBracketError, create a token of fallback_token_type.
Then create a Node from that token and append it to the Analyzer's buffer

functions.name: sqlfmt.actions.add_comment_to_buffer
documentation:
Create a COMMENT token from the match, then create a Comment
from that token and append it to the Analyzer's buffer

functions.name: sqlfmt.actions.add_jinja_comment_to_buffer
documentation:
Create a COMMENT token from the match, then create a Comment
from that token and append it to the Analyzer's buffer; raise
StopRulesetLexing to revert to SQL lexing

functions.name: sqlfmt.actions.handle_newline
documentation:
When a newline is encountered in the source, we typically want to create a
new line in the Analyzer's line_buffer, flushing the node_buffer and
comment_buffer in the process.

However, if we have lexed a standalone comment, we do not want to create
a Line with only that comment; instead, it must be added to the next Line
that contains Nodes

functions.name: sqlfmt.actions.handle_semicolon
documentation:
When we hit a semicolon, the next token may require a different rule set,
so we need to reset the analyzer's rule stack, if new rules have been
pushed

functions.name: sqlfmt.actions.handle_ddl_as
documentation:
When we hit "as" in a create function or table statement,
the following syntax should be parsed using the main (select) rules,
unless the next token is a quoted name.

functions.name: sqlfmt.actions.handle_closing_angle_bracket
documentation:
When we hit ">", it could be a closing bracket, the ">" operator,
or the first character of another operator, like ">>". We need
to first assume it's a closing bracket, but if that raises a lexing
error, we need to try to match the source again against the operator
rule, to get the whole operator token

functions.name: sqlfmt.actions.handle_set_operator
documentation:
Mostly, when we encounter a set operator (like union) we just want to add
a token with a SET_OPERATOR type. However, EXCEPT is an overloaded
keyword in some dialects (BigQuery) that support `select * except (fields)`.
In this case, except should be a WORD_OPERATOR

functions.name: sqlfmt.actions.handle_number
documentation:
We don't know if a token like "-3" or "+4" is properly a unary operator,
or a poorly-spaced binary operator, so we have to check the previous
node.

functions.name: sqlfmt.actions.handle_reserved_keyword
documentation:
Reserved keywords can be used in most dialects as table or column
names without quoting if they are qualified.
https://github.com/tconbeer/sqlfmt/issues/599

This checks if the previous token is a period, and if so, lexes
this token as a name; otherwise this action executes the passed
action (which likely adds the node as some kind of keyword).

functions.name: sqlfmt.actions.handle_nonreserved_top_level_keyword
sqlfmt.actions.lex_ruleset
documentation: Makes a nested call to analyzer.lex, with the new ruleset activated.

functions.name: sqlfmt.actions.handle_jinja_block_start
documentation: Lex tags like {% if ... %} and {% for ... %} that open a jinja block

functions.name: sqlfmt.actions.handle_jinja_block_keyword
documentation: Lex tags like {% elif ... %} and {% else %} that continue an open jinja block

functions.name: sqlfmt.actions.handle_jinja_data_block_start
documentation:
Lex tags like {% set foo %} and {% call my_macro %} that open a jinja block
that can contain arbitrary data.

This can get called from the JINJA ruleset, in which case we need to
raise an additional StopRulesetLexing after the JINJA_DATA segment
is fully lexed.

functions.name: sqlfmt.actions.handle_jinja_block_end
documentation: Lex tags like {% endif %} and {% endfor %} that close an open jinja block

functions.name: sqlfmt.actions.handle_jinja
documentation:
Lex simple jinja statements and expressions (with possibly nested curlies)
and add to buffer

functions.name: sqlfmt.actions.handle_potentially_nested_tokens
documentation:
Lex potentially nested statements, like jinja statements or
c-style block comments

functions.name: sqlfmt.segment.create_segments_from_lines
documentation:
A segment is a list of consecutive lines that are indented from the
first line.

This method takes a list of lines and returns a list of segments.

Is is basically an unfold/corecursion, but due to recursion limits
we need to do it as a loop.

methods.name: sqlfmt.segment.Segment.head
documentation:
Returns the first nonblank line in the Segment, and the index
of that line

methods.name: sqlfmt.segment.Segment.tail
documentation:
Returns the last nonblank line in the Segment, and the index
of that line (from the bottom. TODO: make the index more obvious)

methods.name: sqlfmt.segment.Segment.tail_closes_head
documentation:
Returns True only if the last line in the segment closes a bracket or
simple jinja block that is opened by the first line in the segment.

methods.name: sqlfmt.segment.Segment.split_after
documentation:
Takes an index, and returns a list of either one or two segments,
composed of the lines of self.lines[idx+1:], depending on whether the segment
ends with a closing bracket

methods.name: sqlfmt.formatter.QueryFormatter._split_lines
documentation:
Splits lines to make line depth consistent and syntax
apparent

methods.name: sqlfmt.formatter.QueryFormatter._format_jinja
documentation:
Formats the contents of jinja tags (the code between
the curlies) by mutating existing jinja nodes

methods.name: sqlfmt.formatter.QueryFormatter._merge_lines
documentation:
Merge lines to minimize vertical space used by the
query, while maintaining the syntax hierarchy achieved
by the splitter

methods.name: sqlfmt.formatter.QueryFormatter._dedent_jinja_blocks
documentation:
Jinja block tags, like {% if foo %} and {% endif %}, shouldn't
be printed at their depth, since their contents may be dedented
farther. This dedents the tags as necessary, in a single pass

methods.name: sqlfmt.formatter.QueryFormatter._remove_extra_blank_lines
documentation:
A query can have at most 2 consecutive blank lines at depth (0,0)
and 1 consecutive blank line at any other depth. See issue #249
for motivation and details.

methods.name: sqlfmt.formatter.QueryFormatter.format
documentation:
Applies 4 transformations to a Query:
1. Splits lines
2. Formats jinja tags
3. Dedents jinja block tags to match their least-indented contents
4. Merges lines
5. Removes extra blank lines

classes.name: sqlfmt.query.Query
documentation:
A Query is a collection of Lines, the corresponding raw source string, and
the desired line length. Queries are mutated by the Formatter

classes.name: sqlfmt.mode.Mode

methods.name: sqlfmt.mode.Mode.color
documentation:
There are 4 considerations for setting color:
1. The --force-color option
2. The --no-color option
3. The NO_COLOR environment variable
4. The default behavior, which is to colorize output

This property checks these flags, in descending order of priority,
and sets the authoritative flag accordingly

See no-color.org for details.

A Mode is a container for all sqlfmt config, including formatting config and
report config. For more info on each option, see cli.py

classes.name: sqlfmt.comment.Comment

methods.name: sqlfmt.comment.Comment.__str__
documentation:
Returns the contents of the comment token plus a trailing newline,
without preceding whitespace, with a single space between the marker
and the comment text.

methods.name: sqlfmt.comment.Comment._get_marker
documentation:
For a comment, returns a tuple.

The first element is the comment's marker, which is the symbol or symbols
that indicates that the rest of the token is a comment; e.g., "--" or "#"

The second element is the position of the comment's text, which is the
first non-whitespace character after the marker

methods.name: sqlfmt.comment.Comment._rewrite_marker
sqlfmt.comment.Comment._comment_parts
documentation:
For a comment, returns a tuple of the comment's marker and its contents
(without leading whitespace)

methods.name: sqlfmt.comment.Comment.is_multiline
documentation: Returns True if this Comment contains newlines

methods.name: sqlfmt.comment.Comment.render_inline
documentation: Renders a comment as an inline comment, assuming it'll fit.

methods.name: sqlfmt.comment.Comment.render_standalone
documentation:
For a Comment, returns the string for properly formatting this Comment
as a standalone comment (on its own line)

methods.name: sqlfmt.comment.Comment._split_before
documentation:
When rendering very long comments, we try to split them at the desired line
length and wrap them onto multiple lines. This method takes the contents of
a comment (without the marker) and a maximum length, and splits the original
text at whitespace, yielding each split as a stringd

A Comment wraps a token (of type COMMENT), and provides a number of properties and
methods that are used in formatting and printing the query

methods.name: sqlfmt.merger.LineMerger.create_merged_line
documentation:
Returns a new line by merging together all nodes in lines. Raises an
exception if the returned line would be too long, empty, or the nodes in
any of the lines violate the rules in _raise_unmergeable.

methods.name: sqlfmt.merger.LineMerger._extract_components
documentation:
Given a list of lines, return 2 components:
1. list of all nodes in those lines, with only a single trailing newline
2. list of all comments in all of those lines

Raise CannotMergeException if lines contain nodes that cannot
be merged.

methods.name: sqlfmt.merger.LineMerger._raise_unmergeable
documentation:
Raises a CannotMergeException if the node cannot be merged. Otherwise
returns the node

methods.name: sqlfmt.merger.LineMerger.maybe_merge_lines
documentation:
Tries to merge lines into a single line; if that fails,
splits lines into segments of equal depth, merges
runs of operators at that depth, and then recurses into
each segment

Returns a new list of Lines

methods.name: sqlfmt.merger.LineMerger._fix_standalone_operators
documentation:
If the first line of a segment is a standalone operator,
we should try to merge the first two lines together before
doing anything else

methods.name: sqlfmt.merger.LineMerger._maybe_merge_operators
documentation:
Tries to merge runs of segments that start with operators into previous
segments. Operators have a priority that determines a sort of hierarchy;
if we can't merge a whole run of operators, we increase the priority to
create shorter runs that can be merged

methods.name: sqlfmt.merger.LineMerger._segment_continues_operator_sequence
documentation:
Returns true if the first line of the segment is part
of a sequence of operators of priority <= max_priority

methods.name: sqlfmt.merger.LineMerger._try_merge_operator_segments
documentation:
Attempts to merge segments into a single line; if that fails,
recurses at a lower operator priority

methods.name: sqlfmt.merger.LineMerger._maybe_stubbornly_merge
documentation:
We prefer some operators, like `as`, `over()`, `exclude()`, and
array or dictionary accessing with `[]` to be
forced onto the prior line, even if the contents of their brackets
don't fit there. This is also true for most operators that open
a bracket, like `in ()` or `+ ()`, as long as the preceding segment
does not also start with an operator.

This method scans for segments that start with
such operators and partially merges those segments with the prior
segments by calling _stubbornly_merge()

methods.name: sqlfmt.merger.LineMerger._stubbornly_merge
documentation:
Attempts several different methods of merging the last segment in
new_segments and segment. Returns a list of segments that represent the
best possible merger of those segments

classes.name: sqlfmt.exception.SqlfmtError
documentation:
Generic class for an error encountered when formatting
user code. Should be caught by api.run for display to the
user.

classes.name: sqlfmt.exception.SqlfmtConfigError
documentation: Raised during opening or parsing a pyproject.toml file

classes.name: sqlfmt.exception.SqlfmtUnicodeError
documentation:
Raised while reading input if the input cannot be
decoded into a Python string

classes.name: sqlfmt.exception.SqlfmtParsingError
documentation:
Raised during lexing if sqlfmt encounters a token that does
not match any rules

classes.name: sqlfmt.exception.SqlfmtBracketError
documentation:
Raised during lexing if sqlfmt encounters mismatched, unterminated,
or unexpected closing brackets

classes.name: sqlfmt.exception.SqlfmtSegmentError
documentation: Raised during merging if a segment is unexpectedly empty

classes.name: sqlfmt.exception.SqlfmtEquivalenceError
documentation:
Raised during the safety check if the result query does
not lex to the same tokens as the raw query

classes.name: sqlfmt.exception.SqlfmtControlFlowException
documentation:
Generic exception for exceptions used to manage control
flow. Should always be caught by the application and
never raised back to the user

classes.name: sqlfmt.exception.StopRulesetLexing
documentation:
Raised by the Analyzer or one of its actions to indicate
that further lexing should use the previous ruleset

classes.name: sqlfmt.exception.CannotMergeException
documentation:
Raised by the merger if the passed lines cannot be merged
for any reason

classes.name: sqlfmt.analyzer.Analyzer

methods.name: sqlfmt.analyzer.Analyzer.previous_node
documentation: Return the most recently-lexed Node, if it exists

methods.name: sqlfmt.analyzer.Analyzer.previous_line_node
documentation: Return the last node of the last complete line

methods.name: sqlfmt.analyzer.Analyzer.clear_buffers
documentation:
Reset the analyer's node, comment, and line buffers, and its parsing position.
(It is possible for the same analyzer to be used to lex twice, so we need to
reset buffers before lexing begins)

methods.name: sqlfmt.analyzer.Analyzer.write_buffers_to_query
documentation:
Write the contents of self.line_buffer to query.lines,
taking care to flush node_buffer and comment_buffer first

methods.name: sqlfmt.analyzer.Analyzer.parse_query
documentation:
Initialize a parser and parse the source string, return
a structured Query.

methods.name: sqlfmt.analyzer.Analyzer.get_rule
documentation: Return the rule from ruleset that matches rule_name

methods.name: sqlfmt.analyzer.Analyzer.lex_one
documentation:
Repeatedly match Rules to the source_string (at self.pos)
and apply the matched action.

Mutates the analyzer's buffers and pos

methods.name: sqlfmt.analyzer.Analyzer.lex
documentation:
Repeatedly match Rules to the source_string (until the source_string is
exhausted) and apply the matched action.

Mutates the analyzer's buffers

methods.name: sqlfmt.analyzer.Analyzer.search_for_terminating_token
documentation:
Return the ending position of the correct closing bracket that matches
start_rule

The Analyzer is initialized by the dialect specified by the user. The
dialect specifies the list of rules that the Analyzer will attempt
to match to the source string during parsing. The analyzer maintains
buffers of lexed nodes, comments, and lines.

functions.name: sqlfmt.api.format_string
documentation:
Takes a raw query string and a mode as input, returns the formatted query
as a string, or raises a SqlfmtError if the string cannot be formatted.

If mode.fast is False, also performs a safety check to ensure no tokens
are dropped from the original input.

functions.name: sqlfmt.api.run
documentation:
Runs sqlfmt on all files in Collection of Paths (files), using the specified Mode.

Modifies sql files in place, by default. Check or diff Mode do not modify files,
they only create a Report.

If a callback is provided, will execute the callback after each file is formatted.

Returns a Report that can be queried or printed.

functions.name: sqlfmt.api.get_matching_paths
documentation:
Takes a list of paths (files or directories) and a mode as an input, and
yields paths to individual files that match the input paths (or are contained in
its directories) and are not excluded by the mode's exclude glob

functions.name: sqlfmt.api.initialize_progress_bar
documentation:
Return a callable that can be used with api.run to display a progress bar
that updates after each file is formatted.

Pass force_progress_bar to enable the progress bar, even on non-TTY
terminals (this is handy for testing the progress bar).

functions.name: sqlfmt.api._get_included_paths
documentation:
Takes a list of absolute paths (files or directories) and a mode as an input, and
yields paths to individual files that match the input paths (or are contained in
its directories)

functions.name: sqlfmt.api._format_many
documentation:
Runs sqlfmt on all files in a collection of paths, using the specified mode.

If there are multiple paths and the mode allows it, uses asyncio's implementation
of multiprocessing. Otherwise, reverts to single-processing behavior

Returns a list of SqlFormatResults. Does not write formatted Queries back to disk

functions.name: sqlfmt.api._multiprocess_map
documentation:
Using multiple processes, creates a Future for each application of func to
an item in seq, then gathers all Futures and returns the result.

Provides a similar interface to the map() built-in, but executes in multiple
processes.

functions.name: sqlfmt.api._format_one
documentation:
Runs format_string on the contents of a single file (found at path). Handles
potential user errors in formatted code, and returns a SqlfmtResult

functions.name: sqlfmt.api._update_source_files
documentation:
Overwrites file contents at result.source_path with result.formatted_string.

No-ops for unchanged files, results without a source path, and empty files

functions.name: sqlfmt.api._read_path_or_stdin
documentation:
If passed a Path, calls open() and read() and returns contents as
a tuple of strings. The first element is the contents of the file; the
second element is the encoding used to read the file; the third
element is either the utf BOM or an empty string.

If passed Path("-"), calls sys.stdin.read()

functions.name: sqlfmt.api._perform_safety_check
documentation:
Raises a SqlfmtEquivalenceError if re-lexing
the result produces a different set of tokens than
the original.

functions.name: sqlfmt.cache.get_cache_file
documentation: Returns the path to the cache file on disk

functions.name: sqlfmt.cache.load_cache
documentation:
Returns a Cache (a dictionary keyed by file path) by loading
from a pickle saved to disk

functions.name: sqlfmt.cache.check_cache
documentation:
Returns True if the path is in the cache and the cached stats match the
file on disk

functions.name: sqlfmt.cache.write_cache
documentation: Updates cache with results, then dumps cache to disk

functions.name: sqlfmt.cache.clear_cache
documentation: Deletes the cache file on disk, if it exists

functions.name: sqlfmt.cache._get_cache_info
documentation:
Returns a tuple of (modified_time, file_size) for the path; this tuple is
persisted to the cache, and we check the files on disk against this cached
value to determine if we need to format the file again

functions.name: sqlfmt.cache._gen_cache_keys_for_updates
documentation:
Takes an interable of SqlfmtResults and yields paths to files that should
be updated in the cache, based on the result of the sqlfmt run

functions.name: sqlfmt.cache._should_update_cache
documentation:
Takes a single SqlfmtResult and returns True if that result indicates that
the associated file should be updated in the cache

classes.name: sqlfmt.dialect.Dialect

methods.name: sqlfmt.dialect.Dialect.get_rules
documentation: Returns the Dialect's Rules, sorted by priority

methods.name: sqlfmt.dialect.Dialect.initialize_analyzer
documentation: Creates and returns an analyzer that uses the Dialect's rules for lexing

Abstract class for a SQL dialect.

Each dialect should override the RULES dict to define their own grammar. RULES
must have a key "main" that contains the rules for the main lexing loop.

classes.name: sqlfmt.dialect.Polyglot
documentation:
A universal SQL dialect meant to encompass the common usage of at least
Postgres, MySQL, BigQuery Standard SQL, Snowflake SQL, SparkSQL.

functions.name: sqlfmt.cli.sqlfmt
documentation:
sqlfmt formats your dbt SQL files so you don't have to.

The FILES argument can be one or many paths to sql files (or directories),
or use "-" to use stdin.

Exit codes: 0 indicates success, 1 indicates failed check,
2 indicates a handled exception caused by errors in one or more user code files.

https://sqlfmt.com for documentation and more information.

functions.name: sqlfmt.cli.show_welcome_message
documentation:
Prints a nice welcome message for new users who might accidentally
enter `$ sqlfmt` without any arguments

methods.name: sqlfmt.node_manager.NodeManager.create_node
documentation:
Create a Node from a Token. For the Node to be properly formatted,
method call must also include a reference to the previous Node in the
Query (either on the same or previous Line), unless it is the first
Node in the Query.

The Node's depth and whitespace are calculated when it is created
(this does most of the formatting of the Node). Node values are
lowercased if they are simple names, keywords, or statements.

methods.name: sqlfmt.node_manager.NodeManager.raise_on_mismatched_bracket
documentation:
Raise a SqlfmtBracketError if token is a closing bracket, but it
does not match the token in the last_bracket node

methods.name: sqlfmt.node_manager.NodeManager.raise_on_mismatched_jinja_tags
documentation:
Compare the value of token to the start_tag to determine whether token
closes start_tag

methods.name: sqlfmt.node_manager.NodeManager.open_brackets
documentation:
Uses the previous_node and the contents of the current token
to compute the depth of the new node.

Returns two lists, for open_brackets and open_jinja_blocks

methods.name: sqlfmt.node_manager.NodeManager.whitespace
documentation:
Returns the proper whitespace before the token literal, to be set as the
prefix of the Node.

Most tokens should be prefixed by a simple space. Other cases are outlined
below.

methods.name: sqlfmt.node_manager.NodeManager.standardize_value
documentation:
Tokens that are words (not symbols) and aren't jinja
or comments should be lowercased and have any internal
whitespace replaced with a single space

methods.name: sqlfmt.node_manager.NodeManager.disable_formatting
documentation:
Manage the formatting_disabled property for the node to be created from
the token and previous node.

methods.name: sqlfmt.node_manager.NodeManager.append_newline
documentation: Create a new NEWLINE token and append it to the end of line

variables.name: sqlfmt.rule.MAYBE_WHITESPACES
documentation: any whitespace except newline

classes.name: sqlfmt.rule.Rule

class_variables.name: sqlfmt.rule.Rule.priority
documentation: lower get matched first

A lex rule.

When the analyzer lexes the source string, it applies each Rule in turn,
in ascending order of priority.

The Analyzer tries to match the Rule's regex pattern to the current position
in the source string. If there is a match, the Analyzer calls the Rule's
action with the arguments (self [the analyzer], source_string, match). The
Rule's action may mutate the analyzer's buffer (if desired). The action
must return the position in the source_string where the Analyzer should
look for the next match.

functions.name: sqlfmt.node.get_previous_token
documentation:
Returns the token of prev_node, unless prev_node is a
newline or jinja statement, in which case it recurses

classes.name: sqlfmt.node.Node

methods.name: sqlfmt.node.Node.__str__
documentation: Returns the formatted text of this Node

methods.name: sqlfmt.node.Node.__repr__
documentation:
Because of self.previous_node, the default dataclass repr creates
unusable output

methods.name: sqlfmt.node.Node.__len__
documentation: The length of this printed Node, including prefix whitespace, after formatting

methods.name: sqlfmt.node.Node.depth
documentation:
A Node's depth is a key characteristic that determines its indentation in the
formatted query. We use a tuple to track SQL and jinja depth separately, since
SQL depth can change within jinja blocks

methods.name: sqlfmt.node.Node.is_unterm_keyword
documentation: True for Nodes representing unterminated SQL keywords, like select, from, where

methods.name: sqlfmt.node.Node.is_bracket_operator
documentation:
Node is an opening square bracket ("[")
that follows a token that could be a name.

Alternatively, node is an open paren ("(")
that follow an closing angle bracket.

methods.name: sqlfmt.node.Node.is_multiplication_star
documentation:
A lexed TokenType.STAR token can be the "all fields" shorthand or
the multiplication operator. Returns true iff this Node is a multiplication
operator

methods.name: sqlfmt.node.Node.is_the_between_operator
documentation: True if this node is a WORD_OPERATOR with the value "between"

methods.name: sqlfmt.node.Node.has_preceding_between_operator
documentation: True if this node has a preceding "between" operator at the same depth

methods.name: sqlfmt.node.Node.is_the_and_after_the_between_operator
documentation:
True if this node is a BOOLEAN_OPERATOR with the value "and" immediately
following a "between" operator

A Node wraps a lexed Token, but adds many calculated properties and methods that
simplify formatting, including:

previous_node: a reference to the Node that immediately precedes this Node in the
query

prefix: the calculated whitespace (0 or 1 spaces) that should precede this Node
when formatted

value: the properly-capitalized token contents for the formatted query

open_brackets and open_jinja_blocks: a list of Nodes that precede this Node that
refer to open brackets (keywords and parens) or jinja blocks (e.g., {% if foo %})
that increase the syntax depth (and therefore printed indentation) of this Node

formatting_disabled: a list of FMT_OFF tokens that precede this node and prevent
it from being formatted

classes.name: sqlfmt.line.Line

methods.name: sqlfmt.line.Line.__str__
documentation:
A Line is printed in one of three ways:
1. Blank lines are just bare newlines, with no other whitespace
2. Lines where formatting is disabled must use the original lexed token,
   and print exactly what we lexed
3. Concatenate all Nodes and prepend the correct amount of whitespace
   for indentation

Does not include any Comments; for those, use the render_with_comments
method

methods.name: sqlfmt.line.Line.open_brackets
documentation: The brackets open at the start of this Line

methods.name: sqlfmt.line.Line.open_jinja_blocks
documentation: The jinja blocks open at the start of this Line

methods.name: sqlfmt.line.Line.depth
documentation: The depth of the start of this line

methods.name: sqlfmt.line.Line.prefix
documentation:
Returns the whitespace to be printed at the start of this Line for
proper indentation.

methods.name: sqlfmt.line.Line.render_with_comments
documentation:
Returns a string that represents the properly-formatted Line,
including associated comments

methods.name: sqlfmt.line.Line.from_nodes
documentation:
Creates and returns a new line from a list of Nodes. Useful for line
splitting and merging

methods.name: sqlfmt.line.Line.is_too_long
documentation:
Returns true if the rendered length of the line is strictly greater
than max_length, and if the line isn't a standalone long
multiline node

methods.name: sqlfmt.line.Line.closes_bracket_from_previous_line
documentation:
Returns true for a line with an explicit bracket like ")" or "]"
that matches a bracket on a preceding line. False for unterminated
keywords or any lines with matched brackets

methods.name: sqlfmt.line.Line.previous_line_has_open_jinja_blocks_not_keywords
documentation:
Returns true if the previous line is inside a jinja block, but not
after a jinja block keyword, like {% else %}/{% elif %}

methods.name: sqlfmt.line.Line.closes_jinja_block_from_previous_line
documentation:
Returns true for a line that contains {% endif %}, {% endfor %}, etc.
where the matching {% if %}, etc. is on a previous line.

methods.name: sqlfmt.line.Line.closes_simple_jinja_block_from_previous_line
documentation:
Returns true for a line that contains {% endif %}, {% endfor %}, etc.
where the matching {% if %}, etc. is on a previous line. Returns False
for {% else %}/{% elif %} or an {% endif %} that follows an {% else %}/
{% endif %}

methods.name: sqlfmt.line.Line.opens_new_bracket
documentation:
Returns True iff the Nodes in this Line open a new explicit bracket and do
not also close that bracket

class_variables.name: sqlfmt.line.Line.previous_node
documentation: last node of prior line, if any

A Line is a collection of Nodes and Comments that should be printed together, on a
single line.

classes.name: sqlfmt.token.TokenType

class_variables.name: sqlfmt.token.TokenType.JINJA_BLOCK_END
documentation: {% endif %}

class_variables.name: sqlfmt.token.TokenType.JINJA_BLOCK_KEYWORD
documentation: {% else %}

class_variables.name: sqlfmt.token.TokenType.UNTERM_KEYWORD
documentation: Unterminated keyword

Tokens are basic elements of SQL syntax; we only care about a small number of
token types, which correspond to query structure and therefore formatting

classes.name: sqlfmt.token.Token

methods.name: sqlfmt.token.Token.from_match
documentation: Constructs a Token based on a regex match in a source string, and a type.

Representation of a syntactic element. Tokens always reference their position
in the source query, and contain the full text of the line from the source
query. Tokens are immutable, but some operations replace Tokens in order to
update their type, prefix, or token in the formatted result.

classes.name: sqlfmt.jinjafmt.BlackWrapper

methods.name: sqlfmt.jinjafmt.BlackWrapper.format_string
documentation:
Attempt to use black to format source_string to a line_length of max_length.
Return source_string if black isn't installed or it can't parse source_string.

Return a tuple of the formatted string and a boolean that indicates whether
black successfully ran on the string

methods.name: sqlfmt.jinjafmt.BlackWrapper._preprocess_string
documentation:
Takes a jinja source_string and performs some small transformations on it to
make it valid python that black can format:
1. Detects newlines
2. substitutes python keywords like return for safe alternatives like return_
3. substitutes jinja tilde operators with another binary operator

Returns a tuple of the processed string and a NamedTuple of the stats from
pre-processing

methods.name: sqlfmt.jinjafmt.BlackWrapper._replace_reserved_words
documentation:
Replaces python reserved words in source_string when they are used as variables
or function names. Returns a string with the replacements made and a dict of
the number and types of replacements made

methods.name: sqlfmt.jinjafmt.BlackWrapper._replace_tildes
documentation:
Jinja uses ~ as the string concatenation operator, but black cannot parse the
tilde. This method finds another operator to safely replace the tilde with,
and returns a string with the tilde replaced and a dict with the symbol it
was replaced with and the number of replacements made.

methods.name: sqlfmt.jinjafmt.BlackWrapper._postprocess_string
documentation: Translates a formatted python string back to jinja. Undoes some pre-processing

A thin wrapper around black. Tries to import black when
instantiated. Provides a safe interface, format_string

classes.name: sqlfmt.jinjafmt.JinjaTag

methods.name: sqlfmt.jinjafmt.JinjaTag._multiline_str
documentation:
if the formatted code is on multiple lines, we want the code indented
four spaces past the opening and closing markers. The opening marker
will already be indented to the proper depth (because of the Line).

methods.name: sqlfmt.jinjafmt.JinjaTag._find_multiline_python_str_lines
documentation:
Return a set line numbers that correspond with the lines
of a triple-quoted multiline string (except for the first line
of each string). These are lines that should never have
their indentation adjusted

methods.name: sqlfmt.jinjafmt.JinjaTag._remove_trailing_comma
documentation:
dbt Jinja doesn't allow trailing commas in macro definitions. Mutates
self.code so that it doesn't contain a trailing comma inside parentheses.

methods.name: sqlfmt.jinjafmt.JinjaTag.from_string
documentation:
Takes a jinja statement or expression as a string and returns
a JinjaTag object (basically a tuple of its parts).

methods.name: sqlfmt.jinjafmt.JinjaTag.max_code_length
documentation:
For a tag with max_length remaining characters on the line, return the
max length that the code inside the curlies (exc. the verb) can occupy.

A simple representation of a jinja tag.

"verb" is one of {set, do, for, if, elif, else, test, macro, materialization, call}

For example, "{%- set my_var=4 %}" is split into it parts:
(opening_marker, verb, code, closing_marker) = ("{%-", "set", "my_var=4", "%}")

classes.name: sqlfmt.jinjafmt.JinjaFormatter

methods.name: sqlfmt.jinjafmt.JinjaFormatter.format_line
documentation:
Format each jinja tag in a line, in turn. If a node was made multiline,
split before the node (unless it is already the first node on that line)

methods.name: sqlfmt.jinjafmt.JinjaFormatter._format_jinja_node
documentation:
Format a single jinja tag. No-ops for nodes that
are not jinja. Returns True if the node was blackened

Provides a simple interface, format_line, to format all jinja tags
on a Line, using black if it is installed

methods.name: sqlfmt.splitter.LineSplitter.maybe_split
documentation:
Evaluates a line for splitting. If line matches criteria for splitting,
returns a list of new lines; otherwise returns a list of only the original line.

We used to do this recursively, but very long lines (with >500 splits) would
raise RecursionError.

methods.name: sqlfmt.splitter.LineSplitter.maybe_split_before
documentation: Return True if we should split before node

methods.name: sqlfmt.splitter.LineSplitter.maybe_split_between_brackets
documentation:
Return true if this is an open bracket that follows
a closing bracket. This is typically for BQ
array indexing, like split(my_field)[offset(1)],
or dictionary accessing, like my_json['outer']['inner']

methods.name: sqlfmt.splitter.LineSplitter.maybe_split_after
documentation:
Return True, False if we should always split after node
Retrun False, True if we should never split after node
Return False, False if splitting after should depend on the
contents of the next node

methods.name: sqlfmt.splitter.LineSplitter.split_at_index
documentation:
Return a new line comprised of the nodes line[head:index], plus a newline node.

Also return a list of comments that need to be included in the tail
of the split line. This includes inline comments and if the head
is just a comma, all comments.

functions.name: sqlfmt.config.load_config_file
documentation:
files is a list of resolved, absolute paths (like the ones passed from the
Click CLI). This finds a pyproject.toml file in the common parent directory
of files (or in the common parent's parents).

functions.name: sqlfmt.config._get_common_parents
documentation:
For a list of absolute paths, returns a Set of paths for all
of the common parents of files

functions.name: sqlfmt.config._find_config_file
documentation:
Given an ordered list of directories, returns the path to a
pyproject.toml file in the lowest (most specific) directory

Returns None if no file exists

functions.name: sqlfmt.config._load_config_from_path
documentation:
Loads a toml file located at config path. Returns the contents
under the [tool.sqlfmt] section as a dict.
